title: Cat Overgrown Claws Curling Into Paw Pads: Triage & Care Guide
meta_desc: Learn what to do when your cat's claws curl into their paw pads. Understand the urgency, signs of infection, and how to help your pet comfortably.
content_html:
TL;DR: Ingrown claws occur when a cat's nail grows into the paw pad, causing significant pain and a high risk of infection; most cases require veterinary care within 24 to 48 hours.

What are ingrown cat claws and why do they happen?

Overgrown claws occur when a cat's nails are not regularly worn down or trimmed, leading them to grow in a long, circular arc. Because cat claws are naturally curved and sharp, they can eventually loop back and pierce the sensitive fleshy part of the paw pad. This is most common in senior cats who are less active or those with health conditions that cause the nails to become thick and brittle.

Is an ingrown claw considered a veterinary emergency for my cat?

  • Urgency Level: Medium. While rarely life-threatening, it is a very painful condition that poses a high risk of bacterial infection (abscess).
  • Standard Care: If the claw has already broken the skin or is embedded in the pad, your cat should be seen by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Immediate Care: Seek urgent care if you notice significant swelling, foul-smelling discharge, or if your cat is refusing to walk.

How does providing a photo of my cat's paw help with veterinary triage?

  • Depth Assessment: A photo allows the triage team to see exactly how deep the nail has penetrated the paw pad.
  • Infection Screening: Veterinary professionals can check for signs of secondary infection like redness, swelling, or pus.
  • Clinic Preparation: This information helps determine how quickly your cat needs to be seen and whether the team needs to prepare sedation or specific equipment to safely remove the nail and treat the wound.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Overgrown claws, especially when curling into the paw pads, require careful attention. The strategy is to shorten the claw as much as possible without causing bleeding. After shortening, any horn wall displaced beneath the claw should be carefully cut away. If loose horn is present and causing pain, it should be removed with pincers, potentially requiring regional anesthesia.

Chapter: Musculoskeletal

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1086)

faqs.question: Can I trim the embedded nail at home?
faqs.answer: If the nail has already punctured the paw pad, it is generally not recommended to trim it at home. Removing an embedded nail can cause significant pain and bleeding, and the wound will likely require professional cleaning and antibiotics.

faqs.question: How can I tell if the paw pad is infected?
faqs.answer: Signs of infection include localized heat, redness, swelling, a foul odor, or yellow/green discharge from the site of the puncture. Your cat may also excessively lick the area or limp.

faqs.question: Why did my cat's nails grow so long suddenly?
faqs.answer: As cats age, they often become less active and use scratching posts less frequently. Additionally, certain conditions like hyperthyroidism or simply the aging process can cause the nails to grow thicker and faster, making them more prone to curling.
